narrator.personality: Platform reliability engineer. Calm, clinical. Measures impact in patient appointments affected and provider schedule accuracy. Notes the deploy red herring without leading.

glossary.ElastiCache: A managed in-memory data store service from AWS. It runs Redis or Memcached so your application can read frequently-accessed data from memory instead of querying a database every time.
glossary.Redis: An open-source, in-memory key-value store used for caching, session management, and real-time data. ElastiCache runs Redis as a managed service.
glossary.maxmemory-policy: A Redis configuration setting that controls what happens when the cache reaches its memory limit. It determines whether Redis evicts old keys to make room or rejects new writes entirely.
glossary.noeviction: A maxmemory-policy value where Redis refuses to accept any new write commands once memory is full. Existing data is preserved, but the application receives OOM errors on every write attempt.
glossary.allkeys-lru: A maxmemory-policy value where Redis automatically removes the least recently used keys across all keys to free memory for new writes. The standard recommendation for caching workloads.
glossary.OOM: Out of Memory. An error Redis returns when it cannot allocate more memory for a write command. With noeviction, this happens as soon as used_memory reaches maxmemory.
glossary.cache-aside pattern: A caching strategy where the application checks the cache first. On a hit, it returns cached data. On a miss, it queries the database, stores the result in the cache, and then returns it.
glossary.BytesUsedForCache: A CloudWatch metric that reports how many bytes of memory the ElastiCache node is using for cached data. Used to detect when the cache is approaching its memory ceiling.
glossary.connection pool: A fixed set of pre-opened database connections shared by application threads. When all connections are in use, new requests must wait. The pool size is a hard limit on concurrent database access.
glossary.TTL: Time to Live. A value set on a cache key that tells Redis to automatically delete the key after a specified number of seconds. Keys without a TTL persist until manually deleted or evicted.

narrative_arc.call: Appointment booking pages are loading slowly. Provider offices are calling support about missing availability slots. The booking success rate dropped from 99.2% to 61%.
narrative_arc.threshold: The product team points to the deploy from two hours ago. They want a rollback. But the symptoms started gradually, not in a single moment.
narrative_arc.trials: A deploy went out at 07:15 that added lab result caching. The targets are healthy. The application code is correct. The failure is somewhere between the application and its data layer.
narrative_arc.revelation: Redis memory is at 100%. The maxmemory-policy is noeviction. Every write returns an OOM error, logged at DEBUG. The cache-aside pattern silently falls through to PostgreSQL for every request, saturating the connection pool.
narrative_arc.return: Changing maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru restores cache writes immediately. Adding CloudWatch alarms on BytesUsedForCache and CacheMisses prevents the blind spot from recurring.

components.name: ALB (trellis-prod-alb)
components.role: Receives HTTPS traffic from patients and provider offices and distributes it to the ECS cluster
components.failure_impact: If the ALB is unhealthy, patients and providers cannot access the appointment booking system

components.name: ECS cluster (trellis-prod)
components.role: Runs three tasks of the appointment-service container that implement the cache-aside pattern
components.failure_impact: If tasks fail, appointment booking and provider schedule queries are unavailable

components.name: ElastiCache Redis node (trellis-redis-prod, cache.r6g.large, 13.07 GB maxmemory)
components.role: Stores hot data including doctor availability, session tokens, and appointment slot computations with noeviction policy
components.failure_impact: When memory is full with noeviction, all writes return OOM errors and the application falls through to the database for every request

components.name: RDS PostgreSQL (trellis-prod-db, db.r6g.xlarge, connection pool max 200)
components.role: Serves as the persistent data store for appointments, provider schedules, and patient data
components.failure_impact: When all 200 connections are saturated by cache-miss fallback traffic, query latency spikes and new requests are queued

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Collects ElastiCache metrics (BytesUsedForCache, CacheHits, CacheMisses, Evictions) and RDS metrics (DatabaseConnections, CPUUtilization)
components.failure_impact: Without alarms on BytesUsedForCache and CacheMisses, memory exhaustion goes undetected until users report slow bookings

system_narration.data_flow: Patients and providers send requests through the ALB to the ECS appointment-service. The service uses a cache-aside pattern: it checks Redis first, returns on a hit, and falls through to PostgreSQL on a miss. After a database read, the result is written back to Redis for future requests. CloudWatch collects metrics from both ElastiCache and RDS every 60 seconds.
system_narration.what_broke: The Redis node exhausted its 13.07 GB of maxmemory. With maxmemory-policy set to noeviction, Redis returned OOM errors on all write commands (SET, HSET, LPUSH). The application caught these errors at DEBUG level and silently fell through to PostgreSQL for every request. The RDS connection pool (200) saturated. Query latency went from 5ms to 800ms. ALB TargetResponseTime climbed to 8.2 seconds. The deploy at 07:15 consumed the last 400 MB of headroom but did not introduce the vulnerability.

story_beats.message: Appointment booking success rate dropped from 99.2% to 61%. Patients are retrying, which adds more load. Three provider offices called support about missing availability slots.
The product team is certain it is the deploy from two hours ago. They want to roll back. The deploy added lab result caching. But the symptoms started gradually, not all at once.
Database CPU is at 89%. Connection pool is at capacity. If we do not resolve this soon, the database will become the next failure point.
The deploy is a red herring. The code changes are correct. Look at what is happening between the application and the cache layer.

hints.text: The application is slower than usual. Where does it normally get its data from?
What is the current state of the Redis cache? Is it serving requests normally?
Check the ElastiCache memory metrics. How much memory is in use versus available?

Redis memory is at 100%. The maxmemory-policy is set to noeviction. When memory is full with noeviction, Redis returns OOM errors on write commands. The application falls through to the database for every read.

Fix: change maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru to allow Redis to evict least-recently-used keys. Then add CloudWatch alarms on BytesUsedForCache and Evictions metrics. Consider scaling to a larger node or enabling cluster mode.

resolution.root_cause: The ElastiCache Redis node reached maximum memory (13.07 GB) with maxmemory-policy set to 'noeviction', causing Redis to return OOM errors on all write commands -- the application silently fell through to direct database queries for every request, saturating the RDS connection pool and increasing ALB TargetResponseTime to 8 seconds.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that Redis memory is at 100% capacity and write commands are failing with OOM errors
Player identifies that maxmemory-policy is set to 'noeviction', preventing Redis from freeing memory by evicting old keys
Player proposes changing maxmemory-policy to 'allkeys-lru' or similar eviction strategy
Player proposes adding CloudWatch alarms on BytesUsedForCache, Evictions, and CacheMisses metrics

learning_objectives: Redis eviction policies: 'noeviction' rejects writes when memory is full; 'allkeys-lru' evicts least-recently-used keys to make room for new writes
Cache-aside pattern failure modes: when the cache layer fails, applications must handle the fallback gracefully and log cache failures at an appropriate level
ElastiCache monitoring: BytesUsedForCache, CurrItems, Evictions, CacheMisses, and EngineCPUUtilization are critical metrics for early detection
sop_steps: Check whether the cache has run out of memory. Look at the BytesUsedForCache metric in CloudWatch (it tracks how much memory the cache is using) or connect to the Redis node and run INFO MEMORY. Compare the used_memory value against maxmemory (the hard memory ceiling).

Find out what Redis is configured to do when memory is full. Check the ElastiCache parameter group for the maxmemory-policy setting. This setting controls whether Redis makes room by removing old data or simply refuses new writes. In this case, it is set to noeviction -- meaning Redis rejects all new writes with an Out of Memory (OOM) error.

Apply an immediate fix. Connect to the Redis node and run CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru. This tells Redis to start removing the least recently used data to make room for new writes. The change takes effect instantly but does not survive a reboot.

Make the fix permanent. Update the ElastiCache parameter group so the setting persists across reboots: aws elasticache modify-cache-parameter-group --cache-parameter-group-name trellis-redis-params --parameter-name-values ParameterName=maxmemory-policy,ParameterValue=allkeys-lru

Verify recovery. Watch the CacheHits, CacheMisses, and BytesUsedForCache metrics for 10-15 minutes. You should see memory usage drop as Redis evicts old keys, and the cache hit rate (the percentage of requests served from cache instead of the database) should climb back up.

Fix the application logging. The app was catching Redis OOM errors at DEBUG level, which means they were effectively invisible. Change the log level to WARN so these errors show up in operational dashboards and log searches.

Set up early warning alarms. Create CloudWatch alarms on BytesUsedForCache (alert at 80% of maxmemory) and on CacheMisses (alert on a sustained spike). Send both to an SNS topic so the team is notified before the cache fills up again.

Consider scaling the cache. Options include upgrading to a larger node (cache.r6g.xlarge gives 26.32 GB instead of 13.07 GB), enabling cluster mode (which spreads data across multiple nodes, called horizontal sharding), or adding a read replica for read-heavy workloads.

Audit cache key lifetimes. Check all cache key patterns for explicit TTL values. A TTL (Time to Live) tells Redis to automatically delete a key after a set number of seconds. Keys without TTLs accumulate forever and will eventually fill the cache again.

related_failure_modes.scenario: All requests hit the database at once after a cache restart (thundering herd)
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: When a Redis node restarts or switches to a backup, the cache starts empty. Every single request becomes a cache miss at the same time, slamming the database with a sudden spike instead of a gradual increase. The eviction policy does not matter here because the problem is an empty cache, not a full one.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Turn on data persistence so Redis can reload its data from disk after a restart. Redis supports two options: append-only file (AOF), which logs every write, and snapshots, which save the full dataset periodically. On the application side, use request coalescing or stampede locks -- techniques that prevent hundreds of identical database queries when many requests ask for the same missing cache key at once.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The eviction policy only removes keys with expiration times, but most keys have none
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The eviction policy is set to volatile-lru, which tells Redis to only remove keys that have a TTL (an expiration timer) set. If most keys were stored without a TTL, Redis cannot remove them to free space, and it returns OOM errors just like noeviction would. It looks like eviction is enabled, but in practice nothing can be evicted.
related_failure_modes.prevention: If using volatile-lru, make sure every cache key has an explicit TTL set by the application. Or switch to allkeys-lru, which lets Redis remove any key regardless of whether it has a TTL.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Memory fragmentation makes Redis run out of system memory before reaching its configured limit
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Redis tracks its own memory usage (used_memory), but the operating system may be using significantly more actual memory (used_memory_rss) due to fragmentation -- gaps left behind when data of different sizes is written and deleted over time. The node runs out of real system memory even though Redis thinks it has room. CloudWatch might not show BytesUsedForCache at 100%, making this harder to spot.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Monitor the mem_fragmentation_ratio in the Redis INFO output. If it consistently exceeds 1.5, enable the activedefrag setting in the parameter group, which tells Redis to reorganize memory in the background. Scheduling periodic restarts during maintenance windows can also help.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The cache node itself goes down entirely, not just out of memory
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of memory exhaustion, the Redis node becomes completely unavailable -- hardware failure, network issue, or the entire Availability Zone (a physical data center section) goes offline. All cache operations fail with connection errors, not OOM errors. The cascade to the database is the same, but the fix is failover (switching to a backup node), not changing configuration.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Deploy ElastiCache with at least one read replica in a different Availability Zone and turn on Multi-AZ automatic failover. This means if one node goes down, AWS automatically switches traffic to the backup with minimal interruption.

sop_practices: When you first set up a cache for a caching workload, configure the eviction policy (maxmemory-policy) to allkeys-lru or allkeys-lfu. These policies tell Redis to automatically remove the least recently (or least frequently) used data when memory is full. The noeviction setting should only be used when losing cached data is unacceptable and you are certain the cache will never run out of memory.

Set up CloudWatch alarms on BytesUsedForCache (memory usage) and CacheMisses (how often the cache does not have what the app needs) for every ElastiCache node when you first create it -- not after the first Out of Memory incident.

Log cache write failures at WARN or ERROR level in the application, not DEBUG. When a cache-aside pattern (check cache first, fall back to database on miss) swallows errors at DEBUG level, the cache becomes invisible infrastructure -- it can completely stop working and nobody notices because the errors are hidden.

Set explicit TTLs (expiration timers) on all cache keys and review them quarterly. A TTL tells Redis to automatically delete a key after a set time. Without TTLs, keys accumulate forever, and data growth will eventually fill the cache again.
